text: The laparoscopic view shows the gallbladder positioned beneath the liver within the abdominal cavity.
The surgeon uses dissectors to manipulate and separate tissues during the laparoscopic cholecystectomy procedure.
The surgeon uses a fundus holder to grasp the gallbladder fundus, stabilizing it for further manipulation during the laparoscopic cholecystectomy.
The surgeon uses a Maryland curved dissector to manipulate the gallbladder during the laparoscopic cholecystectomy procedure.

The surgeon begins peeling off the fat and peritoneum from the junction of the gallbladder and cystic duct, dissecting the peritoneum over the cystic artery anteriorly. The dissection continues as the surgeon carefully removes the fat and peritoneum.
The surgeon delineates the anatomy at the junction of the cystic duct, which connects the gallbladder to the common bile duct visible below.
The surgeon uses a Maryland curved dissector to peel off the peritoneum from the junction of the gallbladder and cystic duct, continuing the dissection process.
The surgeon clears the layer of fat around the junction of the gallbladder and cystic duct, ensuring a clear view before applying clips. The surgical field experiences temporary fogging.

The surgeon clears the fog from the camera and re-enters the surgical area, using a blunt dissector and fundus holder to grasp the gallbladder fundus. The posterior dissection involves peeling the gallbladder away from the liver fossa to increase the exposed area.
The surgeon carefully dissects anteriorly near the junction of the gallbladder and cystic duct, taking care to preserve the underlying common bile duct visible below.
The surgeon carefully navigates the inflamed area between the cystic artery and the cystic duct, successfully entering the space while noting the presence of associated lymph nodes.
The surgeon encounters a thickened and slippery gallbladder due to chronic inflammation and surrounding fat, making the dissection challenging.
The surgeon performs the procedure under general anesthesia, with the abdomen visibly inflated using carbon dioxide for laparoscopic access.

The surgeon performs a posterior dissection, focusing on the area above the gallbladder, resembling a fundus-first approach. The dissection technique involves a quadrangle dissection, differing from the traditional Calot's triangle dissection.
The surgeon performs dissection in the posterior window, focusing on the area between the cystic duct, cystic artery, and the base of the gallbladder junction.
The surgeon identifies a cholecystohepatic duct, an additional duct that connects directly from the liver to the gallbladder, distinct from the common bile duct.
The surgeon coagulates a visible vein before tearing it to prevent bleeding, which could obscure the surgical field and necessitate suction to maintain clear visibility.
The surgeon carefully peels off the fat and peritoneum from the cystic artery, which is visibly leading to the gallbladder. A small vessel, possibly a cystic vein, is coagulated during the procedure.
The cystic artery is visibly positioned on the surface of the gallbladder, demonstrating its anatomical course as it supplies the gallbladder.
The surgeon identifies the cystic artery and cystic duct, highlighting the junction between the cystic duct and the common bile duct, which is positioned horizontally below.
The surgeon is positioned at the base of the cystic structure, with the cystic artery visible vertically in the surgical field.
The surgeon visually identifies the junction between the cystic duct and the horizontally lying common bile duct, highlighting the anatomical relationship between these structures.
The video shows a clear demonstration of the junction between the vertically oriented cystic duct and the horizontally lying common bile duct.
The surgeon clips the cystic artery and cystic duct, securing these vessels to prevent bile leakage during the cholestectomy procedure.
The surgeon removes the Maryland dissector and prepares to insert the clip applicator through the surgical port.
The surgeon uses a clip applicator to secure a clip onto the cystic duct and artery, preparing for ligation.
The surgeon applies a clip onto the base of the cystic artery, ensuring it is securely pressed to maintain the structure and prevent fluid leakage.
The surgeon ensures that the clips applied to the cystic duct are secure, squeezing out tissue fluid and blood to prevent the clips from becoming loose.

The surgeon identifies the cystic duct, which is positioned vertically and horizontally adjacent to the common bile duct. The gallbladder is relaxed to ensure the clip is applied correctly onto the cystic duct, avoiding the common bile duct.
The surgeon applies a clip securely onto the cystic duct, ensuring it is positioned away from the common bile duct.
The surgeon prepares to apply another clip onto the cystic duct, ensuring it is positioned away from the common bile duct.
The surgeon uses a single-clip applicator to securely apply a clip onto the cystic duct, ensuring that the clip is properly positioned and secured.
The surgeon applies clips to the cystic duct and cystic artery, preparing to cut these structures to detach the gallbladder from the common bile duct.
The surgeon visually identifies the cystic duct, preparing for further surgical manipulation.

The surgeon focuses on the cystic artery, clearly visualizing and dissecting both the anterior and posterior branches. Careful delineation of the posterior branch is emphasized to prevent potential bleeding.
The surgeon carefully removes the gallbladder from the gallbladder fossa, separating it from the surrounding tissue.
The surgeon uses a monopolar cautery hook to cut and remove the attachments of the gallbladder from both sides, as visible on the screen.
The surgeon pushes up the gallbladder from the liver bed, noting that the posterior wall is not thickly attached despite the thickened fascia and peritoneum on the sides.
The surgeon uses electrocautery to coagulate small capillaries and blood vessels in the fossa where the gallbladder was attached, ensuring hemostasis in the area.
The surgeon performs cauterization on the gallbladder bed, applying heat to coagulate small blood vessels and capillaries in the area.
The gallbladder is successfully separated from the surrounding structures and rests on the gallbladder bed or fossa, with visible cauterization marks from previous dissection.
The gallbladder has been completely removed from the gallbladder bed, leaving the surgical site clear of the organ.
The surgeon uses a suction device to clear fluids and debris from the gallbladder fossa, ensuring a clean surgical field after the gallbladder removal.

The surgeon inspects the gallbladder fossa, ensuring that clips on the cystic duct and artery are properly applied. Any residual blood is suctioned out, confirming there is no bleeding or bile leakage in the area.
The surgeon places a suction drain inside the surgical site, ensuring proper drainage post-procedure.
